weather_line: Ridgelines disputed · coordinates circling · biscuit situation unclear

stories.headline: The Map Speaks First: Christine Sets the Survey Notes on the Table, and the Third Fold Becomes a Plan
body: It happened at half past seven on Thursday morning. Christine placed the survey notes — printed in March, annotated in blue ink, cross-referenced against the brass duck's coordinates with a margin of error she had already calculated and written in red — on the kitchen table, directly on top of Steve's open map. The fold, which had previously been a private theory, became a shared one.

Steve looked at the overlap for eleven seconds. Then he said, 'Seven out of ten becomes eight.' Christine said, 'It becomes a trip.' This exchange has been logged by the Adventure Desk as the formal opening of planning phase for the disputed ridgeline north-northwest of Heron Shelf, provisionally designated the Third Fold Approach and noted on all internal calendars as 'when, not if.'

The 1962 survey dispute remains officially unresolved. The two teams — Halloway & Sons, and the rival firm who refused to be named in the original filing — disagreed by exactly four feet on the ridgeline's elevation at its northern terminus. It is understood that Christine has already identified which team was wrong. She has not said so aloud. She does not need to.

stories.headline: Vault 7 Dispute Enters Its Third Hour; Christine Called; Dispute Ends
body: A disagreement over compound interest, a disputed ledger date, and a goblin supervisor with what colleagues described as 'a very fixed idea about the word retroactive' combined Thursday morning to produce a standoff in the Sub-Vault corridor that three duty liaisons had declined to enter.

Christine arrived at 10:14. By 10:41, the compound interest had been recalculated, the ledger date resolved, and the goblin supervisor was nodding in the precise manner of someone who has been shown, conclusively, that they were right all along — by someone who knew perfectly well they weren't, and had the grace to let them believe it. Account-holders departing the corridor described the air as 'noticeably clearer.' One galleon was ceremonially re-stamped. No further comment was offered.

stories.headline: Orvell Sends a Postcard; Contents Evaluated; Brass Duck Has Been Moved to a Place of Honour
body: A postcard arrived Thursday from Margaret Orvell, retired competitive duck-placer and the woman responsible for what the Investigation Board still has listed as a Regional Incident. The card shows a ridgeline. There is a single word on the back: 'Soon.' The postmark is local, which the Investigation Board considers either reassuring or alarming depending on whom one asks.

The brass duck — red bandana, coordinates on the base, earned fair and square at Heron Shelf — has been relocated from the mantle to the windowsill nearest the map. Steve maintains this was a coincidence of shelf space. No one has challenged this. The postcard has been placed beside it.

stories.headline: Thursday's Creature Report: Internal Movements, a Diplomatic Incident Near the Water Bowl, and One Ongoing Inquiry
body: Sources within the household confirm a morning of significant creature activity, though accounts remain, as ever, contested. The biscuit situation, described on Wednesday as stable, has been reclassified as 'pending review' following an unexplained shortfall in the tin. No formal suspects have been named. Investigators are not optimistic.

familiars_corner: Lucy: Offered a full and unprompted account of her whereabouts between seven and eight a.m., which investigators found thorough, detailed, and completely unverifiable. The biscuit tin was unavailable for comment.

Ellie: Spent a productive forty minutes making friends with a sunbeam that moved. When it disappeared behind a cloud she sat patiently for its return, which she considered a perfectly reasonable plan and still does.

Jersey: Having completed a rigorous morning assessment of available laps, Steve's was selected on grounds of warmth and structural stability. Jersey is now asleep and has asked not to be disturbed regarding either survey disputes or ridgeline elevations.
founding_editor_note: Four feet is not nothing. I once spent a full Saturday re-levelling a garage slab over three-quarters of an inch. They never found the survey marker either, and I was right, which is what matters. — D.

in_brief.sport: Thursday Weightlifting: event postponed due to map consultation; organiser cited 'competing priorities of the interesting kind.'
in_brief.forecast: Clear over the highlands with a strong possibility of departure; four-foot elevation disputes expected to resolve themselves, one way or another, on the ridgeline.

storyline_updates.title: The Third Fold
storyline_updates.summary: Christine placed the survey notes on the map Thursday morning and the planning phase began in earnest. Steve's confidence has risen from seven to eight out of ten. Christine has identified which 1962 survey team was wrong but has not said so aloud. Orvell's postcard ('Soon,' postmarked locally) has arrived, and the brass duck and its coordinates sit beside the map. Day 4 should deliver the departure or the eve of it — the practical preparations, a final look at the coordinates, and the sense that the disputed ridgeline is about to be resolved by the only method that matters: going there.
